did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a neural network classifier for the automatic detection and classification of melanoma (malignant vs. benign) from dermoscopy images. It extracts high-level features based on semantic analysis of shape, color, and texture to train the neural network, achieving a sensitivity of 92.8% and specificity of 95.4% on a database of 206 skin lesion images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design of high-level features for semantic analysis and the use of a neural network classifier for automatic melanoma detection. It details a specific methodology (pre-processing, segmentation, feature extraction, classification), describes the dataset used (206 images from Dermatology Information System and DermQuest), and reports original experimental results (sensitivity of 92.8%, specificity of 95.4%). Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE IPAS'16), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: High-Level features for automatic skin lesions neural network based classification
journal: IEEE IPAS’16: INTERNATIONAL IMAGE PROCESSING APPLICATIONS AND SYSTEMS CONFERENCE 2016
brief_summary: This study addresses the automatic detection and classification of melanoma versus benign skin lesions using dermoscopy images. The authors propose a method that generates high-level features based on semantic analysis of shape, color, and texture to model the ABCD melanoma criteria. A neural network classifier is employed to make the final diagnostic decision based on these extracted features. The experimental evaluation was conducted on a dataset of 206 skin lesion images sourced from the Dermatology Information System and DermQuest databases. The proposed approach achieved a sensitivity of 92.8% and a specificity of 95.4%. Comparative results indicate that this method outperforms several recent works in terms of accuracy and specificity.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection and classification. The study focuses on distinguishing melanoma from benign lesions. The dataset contained 119 melanoma images out of 206 total images.

The comparator class used to distinguish from melanoma. The dataset contained 87 benign nevus images. The paper notes the difficulty in differentiating melanoma from benign nevi due to similar appearance in early stages.

The study aims to differentiate melanocytic from non-melanocytic lesions (Step 1) and then classify melanocytic lesions as benign or malignant (Step 2). The classifier specifically targets melanoma vs. benign nevi within the melanocytic group.

Mentioned as part of the diagnostic process (Step 1: differentiate melanocytic and non-melanocytic lesions), but the experimental classification task focused on melanoma vs. benign nevi. The term 'non-melanoma' was used in comparative studies cited (e.g., C.M.Emre et al.) to refer to benign lesions in the context of melanoma detection.
Mentioned in the introduction as one of the three main types of skin cancer, but not studied or included in the experimental dataset.
Mentioned in the introduction as one of the three main types of skin cancer, but not studied or included in the experimental dataset.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category: Melanoma (malignant) vs. Benign Nevus (benign)
all_task_types.details: The paper employs a neural network classifier to predict discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. benign nevi) based on high-level features derived from dermoscopy images. The task is explicitly defined as 'automatic skin lesions neural network based classification' and 'discrimination of melanocytic skin tumors'. Performance is measured using sensitivity (92.8%) and specificity (95.4%), which are metrics for binary classification.

datasets_used.name: Dermatology Information System and DermQuest
datasets_used.description: A combined dataset of 206 dermoscopy images of skin lesions obtained from two online public databases: Dermatology Information System [15] and DermQuest [16]. The images were captured using standard consumer-grade cameras. This is the same dataset used in reference [6] (Robert Amelard et al.).

use.training: Used for training the neural network classifier. The study employed a random 66%/34% data split for training/testing in each trial.
use.testing: Used for testing the neural network classifier. The remaining 34% of the data was used for testing in each trial.
use.other: Used for comparative analysis against previous works (e.g., Robert Amelard et al., C.M. Emre et al., A.S. Mariam et al., H.S. Ganzeli et al., B. Catarina et al.) to evaluate performance metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to calculate central asymmetry factor and axial shape asymmetry for feature extraction.
Statistically analyzed to extract texture features including contrast, correlation, homogeneity, dissimilarity, and entropy.

statistical_methods_used.method: K-means clustering with intra-class and inter-class variance
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to determine the number of colors and analyze color distribution for feature extraction.
Used for training and testing the neural network classifier in each trial.
Used to evaluate the performance of the neural network classifier and compare with previous works.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy metrics, and compared qualitatively with previous works.

dataset_details.split: Random 66%/34% data split for training/testing in each trial

Dermatology Information System and DermQ.notes: The proposed method outperforms previous works in terms of accuracy and specificity. High-level features performed significantly better than low-level features. The comparative study includes results from other studies that used different datasets (e.g., Mariam et al. used 102 images, Catarina et al. used 176 images, Emre et al. used 914 images), but the primary experimental evaluation of the proposed model was conducted on the 206-image database.

architecture_details.description: A neural network classifier used for decision making based on high-level features. It consists of two processes: training and test. The paper notes it can be considered as a superposed classifier alongside SVM, but the primary implementation is a neural network.
feature_input: Shape asymmetry (Central shape asymmetry, Axial shape asymmetry)
Border (Compactness, Max to min radius signature, Border irregularity)

training_details.data_split: Random 66%/34% data split for training/testing in each trial

preprocessing_techniques: Median filter (nonlinear digital filtering technique used to remove noise while preserving edges)
Thresholding (used for segmentation to obtain a binary image)
Manual segmentation (lesions were manually segmented to provide an 'ideal' segmentation for feature extraction)
conclusions: Semantic analysis using high-level features is a useful method for the discrimination of melanocytic skin tumors.
The proposed neural network-based classification method achieved a sensitivity of 92.8% and a specificity of 95.4% on a database of 206 skin lesion images.
The proposed approach outperforms recent previous works in terms of accuracy and specificity.
High-level features perform significantly better than low-level features for skin lesion classification.
Skin lesion classification accuracy was improved when methods of feature extraction were extended and concatenated with high-level features.
The study highlights the difficulty in differentiating melanoma from benign nevi due to their similar appearance in early stages and the variety of melanoma's shape, color, and texture.
Future work may involve designing high-level features to characterize new features that increase diagnosis accuracy and developing a larger database to meet more significant representative cases.
important_remarks: The dataset is small (206 images) and sourced from public databases (Dermatology Information System and DermQuest) captured with standard consumer-grade cameras, which may limit generalizability to clinical settings using specialized dermatoscopes.
The study relies on manual segmentation for feature extraction, which introduces potential human bias and limits the fully automated nature of the pipeline.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated only on the specific 206-image dataset used for training/testing.

Comparative results against other studies are potentially confounded because those studies used different datasets with varying sizes and compositions (e.g., Emre et al. used 914 images, Mariam et al. used 102 images), making direct performance comparisons difficult.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (p-values) for the performance metrics, relying instead on point estimates of sensitivity and specificity.
The neural network architecture details (e.g., number of layers, neurons, activation functions, hyperparameters) are not explicitly provided in the text.

The study focuses exclusively on binary classification (melanoma vs. benign nevi) and does not address multi-class classification or other skin cancer types like basal cell carcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma.
